food_name: Fried Wonton (Deep-fried Pork and Prawn Wonton)
region_of_origin: Guangdong (Canton), China; widely adopted in Singapore hawker and restaurant culture
food_subgroup: Deep-fried thin wheat flour wonton wrapper with pork and prawn filling

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific concept for Fried Wonton. Closest parent is a deep-fried thin wheat flour pastry with meat filling. No exact FoodOn URI assigned to avoid fabrication.
ontology.foodex2_description: Deep-fried thin wheat flour wrapper with pork and prawn filling (closest parent match under deep-fried cereal-based snack with meat filling)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: A0CEB is the closest defensible FoodEx2 parent for a deep-fried wheat-based snack with mixed meat filling. No exact FoodEx2 code exists for fried wonton specifically.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for fried wonton as a specific food item.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of fried wonton in Singapore is 5 pieces weighing approximately 80g, served without dipping sauce; this delivers approximately 228 kcal, 10g protein, 13.2g fat, and 18g carbohydrate.

nutrient_source.title: Fried Wonton (Deep-fried Pork and Prawn Wonton) — HPB Singapore Foodo
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Fried Wonton (Deep-fried Pork and Prawn Wonton). Core macros and micronutrients are verified against this primary regional source. Minor micronutrients (copper, manganese, vitamin K) supplemented from enriched_json with confidence 0.9.

gi_source.title: International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values — University of Sydney GI Database; wheat flour-based fried pastry analogous food reference
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for fried wonton in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 68 is estimated from the closest analogous food: thin refined wheat flour wrapper (GI range 65–75 for similar fried wheat-based pastries). The deep-frying process and the protein-fat content of the pork-prawn filling moderate the effective GI. GL of 12.2 is calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (68 × 18g) / 100 = 12.24, rounded to 12.2. This is classified as medium GI (56–69 range) and medium GL (11–19 range).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content 480 mg per 100g from soy sauce seasoning in filling
hypertension.guidance: A standard 80g serving (5 pieces) delivers approximately 384 mg sodium; when consumed with a typical 15g sweet chilli dipping sauce (adding ~120 mg sodium), total sodium intake approaches 500 mg per snack occasion, representing approximately 22% of the 2,300 mg daily limit — individuals with hypertension should limit portion size, avoid dipping sauce, and account for this sodium within their daily total.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium GI (68) from refined wheat flour wrapper, moderated by fat and protein in filling; glycemic load 12.2 per 80g serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: The medium glycemic load of 12.2 per 80g serving is manageable within a controlled carbohydrate meal plan; the protein (10g) and fat (13.2g) per serving slow gastric emptying and blunt the postprandial glucose response, but portion control is essential as additional pieces or dipping sauce will increase the glycemic and caloric load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat 16.5g/100g including saturated fat 3.5g/100g and cholesterol 55mg/100g from deep-frying and pork filling
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Deep-frying contributes significant total fat (16.5g/100g) with saturated fat at 3.5g/100g and cholesterol at 55mg/100g; an 80g serving provides approximately 13.2g total fat and 44mg cholesterol, which is moderate — individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit frequency of consumption and pair with lower-fat foods to stay within daily saturated fat targets of less than 20g.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fried Wonton (Deep-fried Pork and Prawn Wonton) is one of Singapore's most popular Chinese appetisers and hawker snacks, originating from Cantonese cuisine in Guangdong, China. Each piece consists of an ultra-thin refined wheat flour wonton wrapper folded around a seasoned filling of minced pork and chopped prawn (shrimp), typically flavoured with ginger, scallions, soy sauce, and sesame oil, then deep-fried in vegetable oil until the wrapper becomes shatteringly crispy, golden, and blistered. The texture contrast between the brittle, airy wrapper and the juicy, tender meat filling is the defining characteristic of this dish. Nutritionally, per 100g it provides 285 kcal, 12.5g protein, 16.5g fat (saturated fat 3.5g), and 22.5g carbohydrate, with 480mg sodium. The medium glycemic index (GI 68) is moderated by the fat and protein content of the filling. Clinically, the sodium content warrants attention for individuals managing hypertension, and the total fat and cholesterol load is relevant for cardiovascular disease management. The glycemic load per 80g serving is 12.2, making it manageable in moderate portions for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. Served universally with sweet chilli sauce, plum sauce, or Worcestershire-soy dipping sauce. A standard serving is 5 pieces (~80g). Distinct from boiled wonton in wonton noodle soup, the fried version has substantially higher fat content due to oil absorption during deep-frying.
common_user_queries: fried wonton vs boiled wonton calories
how many calories in 5 fried wontons

confidence_reason: Core macros and micronutrients sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo, the primary approved regional source for Singapore dishes. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database framework applied to thin refined wheat flour fried pastry, clearly documented. Ontology uses closest-parent matches with honest gap notes. All multilingual names verified.
notes: Trans fat is reported as 0.0g per 100g per the HPB Foodo source, confirmed as negligible/trace from the deep-frying process using standard vegetable oil. Copper (0.08mg), manganese (0.05mg), and vitamin K (0.5mcg) values are supplemented from enriched_json with confidence 0.9 as these micronutrients were not individually itemised in the primary HPB source. Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat breakdown not reported by the primary source; set to -1.0. Water content not reported by primary source; set to -1.0. The duplicate portion entry (two entries for '1 serving 100g') in the input has been resolved to a single default serving of 80g (5 pieces) as the more clinically meaningful portion. Halal status is not confirmed as pork is a primary ingredient; is_halal set to false.
